transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes
What is your main medical concern today?
I have jaw pain.
When did your jaw pain start?
Probably about a week ago.
Can you describe the type of jaw pain you are experiencing and if anything makes it better or worse?
No. Nothing nothing seems to be making it better, but worse if I'm chewing gum or if I eat a bagel. It's it's worse. And when I wake up in the morning, it really is killing me.
Do you have any jaw swelling, redness, or difficulty opening your mouth?
No. But it does make a clicking noise when I open it.
Any recent dental work, trauma, or known arthritis?
No.
Any fever, chills, or recent infections?
No.
Any headaches, ear pain, or facial numbness along with the jaw pain?
I do have a headache, and I intermittently have had ear pain on that side as well. But right now, I don't have a headache, and right now, I don't have ear pain. Just the jaw pain.
Any difficulty chewing, speaking, or jaw locking episodes?
No.
Any history of teeth grinding or clenching, especially at night?
Yes.
Any recent weight loss, night sweats, or unexplained fatigue?
No.
Any pain or tenderness in your temples or jaw when you chew, or any vision changes?
Those are weird questions to ask together, but, no, I I don't have any pain in my temples. Or maybe a little bit when I chew, but I have no vision changes either.
The next few questions may feel repetitive, but they are important for medication safety and treatment decisions. How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
No.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Male
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No. No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No

It sounds like your jaw pain is most likely related to a condition called temporomandibular joint disorder, often linked to teeth grinding. Because this is a phone visit, I can’t examine you or do tests, but based on what you told me, medication like ibuprofen may help with the pain. A licensed physician will review this note and may prescribe the medication if appropriate. For now, you can take acetaminophen or ibuprofen if you can safely do so. If you notice your jaw locking, severe pain worsening, fever, or any new neurological symptoms, please seek in-person care the same day. Otherwise, if symptoms persist or get worse over the next week or two, consider seeing a healthcare provider. This service does not include in-person visits, so if you need an exam or testing, please go to urgent care or your primary care clinician.
